title: GMAT Focus Edition: What Changed and How to Adapt Your Preparation
author: Tklesson Team
content:
The GMAT Focus Edition, launched in 2024, is a significantly different exam from the previous version. If you prepared for the old GMAT or if you are using older preparation materials, you need to understand what has changed. The new exam is shorter, more focused, and tests different skills in different proportions. Here is what is new and how to adjust your preparation accordingly.

What Was Removed and What Was Added

The GMAT Focus Edition removed three components that were part of the previous exam. The Analytical Writing Assessment, which required you to write an essay analyzing an argument, is gone entirely. Sentence Correction, which tested grammar and usage, has been removed from the Verbal Reasoning section. Geometry has been removed from the Quantitative Reasoning section. If you spent time mastering the rules of parallelism or memorizing the properties of circles, that preparation no longer applies to the GMAT.

The most significant addition is the Data Insights section. This section incorporates elements of the old Integrated Reasoning section along with new question types. It tests your ability to interpret data from multiple sources, including charts, graphs, tables, and written passages. The questions require you to synthesize information from different formats and draw conclusions. This section reflects the growing importance of data literacy in business education and management.

The remaining sections have been refined. Quantitative Reasoning now focuses more narrowly on algebra, arithmetic, and word problems. It includes Problem Solving questions, which are familiar, and Data Sufficiency questions, which remain unique to the GMAT but have been reduced in number. Verbal Reasoning focuses exclusively on Critical Reasoning and Reading Comprehension. The removal of Sentence Correction means the Verbal section now tests only your ability to analyze arguments and comprehend written passages.

How the Scoring Changed

The total score on the GMAT Focus Edition ranges from 205 to 805. This is a different scale from the old 200 to 800. A 700 on the Focus Edition represents roughly the same percentile as a 700 on the old exam, but the numbers look different. The score report also provides percentile rankings for each section and for the total score, which is what business schools primarily consider.

Each section is scored from 60 to 90. The three section scores are combined to produce the total score. The scoring algorithm weights your performance across all sections, with no single section dominating the total. This means you cannot compensate for a very weak Quantitative score with a very strong Verbal score to the same degree that you might on other exams. Balanced preparation across all three sections is essential.

How to Adapt Your Study Plan

If you are transitioning from the old GMAT, stop studying Sentence Correction and Geometry immediately. The time you would have spent on those topics should be redirected to Data Insights. This new section is unfamiliar to most test-takers, which means that preparation in this area can give you a competitive advantage. A strong Data Insights score can differentiate you from other applicants who have not adapted their preparation to the new format.

If you are starting fresh, begin with a diagnostic test that reflects the Focus Edition format. The GMAC offers a free practice exam on its website. Take it early to establish your baseline and to familiarize yourself with the new question types. The diagnostic will reveal which sections are your strengths and which need the most work.

For Data Insights, practice interpreting different types of visual data. You should be comfortable extracting information from bar charts, line graphs, pie charts, scatter plots, and tables. You should also practice multi-source reasoning, where information is presented across two or three tabs or sections and you must integrate it to answer questions. These skills are not typically taught in undergraduate programs, so deliberate practice is necessary.

For Quantitative Reasoning, focus on the logic of Data Sufficiency questions. The number of these questions has been reduced, but they remain a distinctive feature of the GMAT and require a unique approach. You are not solving the problem. You are determining whether the information provided is sufficient to solve it. Practice evaluating sufficiency without calculating full solutions.

For Verbal Reasoning, focus on Critical Reasoning. This question type makes up a larger proportion of the Verbal section now that Sentence Correction is gone. Practice identifying conclusions, premises, and assumptions. Practice the negation test for assumption questions. Practice distinguishing between strengthening and weakening answer choices.

What Has Not Changed

The GMAT remains computer-adaptive. The difficulty of the questions you see depends on your performance on previous questions. Early questions in each section carry disproportionate weight. The exam is still taken at a test center or online. The skills it tests, logical reasoning, quantitative analysis, and data interpretation, remain the same skills that business schools value. The format has changed, but the underlying purpose of the exam has not.

The Bottom Line

The GMAT Focus Edition is a shorter, more focused exam that removes Sentence Correction, Geometry, and the Analytical Writing Assessment while adding a Data Insights section. If you prepared for the old GMAT, redirect your study time from the removed topics to Data Insights. If you are starting fresh, take a diagnostic test in the new format and build your preparation around the three current sections. The exam has changed, but the skills it rewards have not. Strategic preparation remains the key to a high score.
